participants.reasoning: The extracted criteria omit or alter several official exclusions (e.g., inability to read/write English, psychosis/suicidal ideation, inpatient psychiatric admission within 2 years, specific endocrine exclusions like androgen-secreting tumour/Cushing syndrome/prolactin >30), and add details not stated officially such as stable medication allowances.
participants.extracted: Eighty-six women aged 18 years or older with body mass index (BMI) ≥25 kg/m2 (overweight or obese) were enrolled from Penn State Hershey Medical Center clinics and advertisements. The sample included 31 women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and women without PCOS; participants were randomized to mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) or health education control. Women were generally allowed to continue stable treatments such as metformin, insulin, antihypertensives, or ovarian suppressive therapy if doses had been stable for at least 6 weeks before enrollment. Key exclusions included pregnancy, secondary causes of hyperandrogenemia, untreated thyroid disease, severe active neuropsychological disorder, severe untreated depression or anxiety without specialist oversight, recent psychiatric hospitalization, active alcohol or drug abuse, inability to communicate in English, inability to commit to the intervention/follow-up, current stress-reduction program participation, recent regular mindfulness practice, other investigative study participation, and type 1 diabetes.
participants.official:
Participants were recruited through Penn State Health Hershey Medical Center clinics and advertisements from November 2011 to December 2013.
Inclusion criteria:
Women, age ≥ 18 years, and BMI ≥ 25 kg/m^2
Exclusion criteria:
Current pregnancy, untreated hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism, type 1 diabetes, androgen secreting tumour, Cushing syndrome, prolactin > 30 ng/mL, severe active neuropsychological disorder, psychosis or suicidal ideation, severe untreated depression or anxiety, inpatient adm

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention and control descriptions match the official record on the 8-week MBSR schedule, retreat, instructor background, daily home practice time, and the attention-matched health education content, with only minor wording differences.
interventions.extracted: Participants randomized to the intervention arm received an 8-week standard Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) program developed by Jon Kabat-Zinn and delivered in weekly 2.5-hour group sessions plus a 6-hour retreat, for a total of 26 hours of instructor-led class time. The program included formal mindfulness practices such as body scan, breath awareness, sitting meditation, standing and floor yoga, walking meditation, and mindfulness in daily activities, along with group discussion and instruction on applying mindfulness to stress reduction. Participants were asked to complete home practice for 25 to 30 minutes per day, 6 days per week, and kept daily practice logs. The intervention was delivered by a trained MBSR instructor with prior professional training and experience, under regular supervision from a senior MBSR instructor, with fidelity monitoring through session fidelity reports and review of selected audio recordings. The control group received an attention-matched health education program over the same 8-week period, with weekly 2.5-hour classes plus a 6-hour retreat led by a registered dietitian. Health education covered PCOS and obesity, symptoms, complications and treatments, diet based on ADA guidelines, exercise, and general stress management; control participants were also asked to spend 25 to 30 minutes per day reviewing course materials and keeping time logs. Both groups continued usual care and were instructed not to start other stress-reduction, exercise, or health education programs during the study.
interventions.official:
Intervention(duration 8 weeks, setting not reported):
Participants randomised to MBSR received the standard MBSR programme consisting of instructor‐led weekly 2.5‐hour sessions for 8 weeks and a 6‐hour retreat session (Kabat‐Zinn 1990), but were asked to do only 25 to 30 minutes of daily home practice instead of the standard 45 minutes. There were no other changes to the standard MBSR curriculum. The instructor had completed professional MBSR training and had 9 years of experience training other

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes list many additional primary and secondary measures and time points beyond the official 16-week follow-up outcomes, which only include a much smaller set of endpoints.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure a primary outcome of mindfulness at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks using the Toronto Mindfulness Scale (TMS), administered after a standardized 15-minute quiet/breathing period at the baseline visit and repeated at follow-up visits. Secondary outcomes were planned at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks and included: blood pressure, specifically mean arterial pressure (MAP) derived from repeated seated systolic and diastolic measurements; glucose metabolism outcomes including HbA1c, fasting glucose, fasting insulin, and HOMA-IR; lipid profile; androgenic hormones including testosterone and DHEAS; inflammation via high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP); salivary cortisol profile including cortisol awakening response, diurnal cortisol slope, waking cortisol, and bedtime cortisol; anthropometrics including weight, height, waist and hip circumferences, and BMI; hirsutism severity using the modified Ferriman-Gallwey score; acne severity using a standardized acne lesion count/diagram assessment; health-related quality of life using the SF-36 in all participants and the PCOSQ in participants with PCOS; psychological distress using the Brief Symptom Inventory-18 (BSI-18), including anxiety and depression subscales; facets of mindfulness using the Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ); sleep outcomes using PROMIS Sleep Disturbance and PROMIS Sleep-Related Impairment; female sexual distress using the Female Sexual Distress Scale (FSDS); perceived stress using the Perceived Stress Scale-10 (PSS-10); positive and negative affect using the PANAS; intervention expectancy using the Stress Reduction Expectancy Scale; perceived outcomes and barriers to adherence at 8 weeks; and feasibility/adherence measures including recruitment, retention, class attendance, home practice, and adverse events.
outcomes.official: Follow‐up at 16 weeks: SBP, DBP, anxiety, depression, perceived stress, FBG, HbA1c, weight, BMI, HRQoL
